short.headline: India's lopsided electricity transition
short.dek: The national average hides about thirty different state paths, from near-zero-carbon to almost pure coal
short.body: India's electricity is getting cleaner nationally, but there is no single Indian grid. In 2024, carbon intensity ranged from near zero in Himalayan hydro states to over 800 gCO2/kWh in the coal belt. The national clean share has climbed from about 17% to over a quarter, real progress, yet coal generation almost quadrupled because demand grew faster than clean supply. The transition is real, but not one transition, and not happening to everyone.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this actually mean, macha?
macha.body: You hear 'India's grid is getting greener.' That's true on average, but your switch doesn't run on the national average. In Himachal, the power is almost entirely from mountain rivers. In Bihar, it's almost pure coal. Over two decades, the clean share has climbed from a sixth to over a quarter. But behind that number, we built enough solar, yet still almost quadrupled coal burning because we needed more of everything. The real story is a handful of states racing to build wind and solar while the coal belt stays locked in. The transition is on, but not where most people live, and the cleanest grids are often the ones that were always clean because of rivers, not new efforts. So be loud about the leaders, and honest about the rest.
macha.soWhat: India is not one grid; it's thirty experiments, and the averages flatter the laggards.

article.title: Is India's electricity getting cleaner, or only in some states?
article.standfirst: Yes, and also not really. India has not one electricity grid getting cleaner, but about thirty, with carbon intensity ranging from near zero in Himalayan hydro states to over 800 gCO2/kWh in the coal belt. The national clean share has climbed from about 17% to over a quarter, real progress, but averaging hides a lopsided transition: coal generation still nearly quadrupled as demand outpaced clean supply. This is one country, running many different energy experiments at once.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## How clean is each state's electricity?

The map shows the carbon intensity of power generation inside each state in 2024. It is not a story of one Indian grid, but about thirty. The colour on a state tells you how many grams of CO2 are released for every kilowatt-hour of electricity produced there. Dark red means coal-heavy; pale blue or green means clean.

At one end, the Himalayan and northeastern states are almost entirely hydro. Himachal Pradesh, Jammu and Kashmir, Sikkim, and Arunachal Pradesh sit near the methodology floor of about 24 gCO2/kWh. That is not a measured zero, it is a constant applied to grids dominated by renewables and nuclear, but it reflects near-zero-carbon generation.

At the other end, the coal belt burns aggressively. Bihar and Jharkhand sit around 815 gCO2/kWh, Chhattisgarh around 806. Those numbers mean that for every unit of power produced inside those states, over 800 grams of CO2 enter the atmosphere. The national average, about 620 gCO2/kWh, sits exactly between the two poles, hiding the entire spread.

It is vital to remember that this is generation, not consumption. A state that hosts a large coal plant may be making power for a neighbouring state, and a state with a clean generating mix may still import dirty power.

## Has India's electricity grid really been getting cleaner nationally?

In the big picture, yes. The clean share of India's electricity generation, renewables plus nuclear, has risen from 16.8% in 2000 to 26.7% in 2025. The line chart shows a genuine, decade-plus climb. The shift is real: a quarter of the country's electricity now comes from low-carbon sources, up from a sixth at the turn of the century.

"Clean" here means hydropower, wind, solar, biomass, and nuclear. Fossil fuels, coal, gas, oil, are the rest. The climb has not been perfectly steady; a sharp spike in 2020 partly reflects the demand dip during the pandemic lockdown. But the direction is unmistakable. India's renewable capacity additions are beginning to register in the generation numbers.

Still, a rising share is a ratio. It does not guarantee that the absolute amount of dirty power is falling. That is the honest complication the next chart reveals.

## If the grid is getting cleaner, why did coal generation keep growing?

Because India needed far more electricity, and clean supply could not keep pace with demand. The multi-line chart shows that from 2000 to 2025, coal generation nearly quadrupled, from roughly 390 TWh to nearly 1,500 TWh. That is not a typo: even as the clean share rose, the absolute amount of coal burned each year surged.

Solar, the brightest spot, climbed from near zero to about 196 TWh. Wind grew from about 1.6 to about 104 TWh. Hydro expanded from about 77 to about 178 TWh, and nuclear from about 16 to about 54 TWh. Gas, the other fossil fuel, actually slipped from about 56 to about 49 TWh. So the fossil growth is almost entirely coal.

The whole pie expanded so fast that even a larger clean slice could not shrink the coal slice. Total generation grew roughly 31% between 2019 and 2024 alone. A rising clean share and growing coal generation are not contradictory; they happen together when demand outruns clean additions.

## Why doesn't a megawatt of solar replace a megawatt of coal?

Because a solar panel does not work at night. The simple reason is capacity factor: the share of the year a plant actually runs at full power. Nuclear runs at 76% capacity factor in India, coal at 69%. They are reliable baseload. Hydro, at 34%, swings with the seasons. Wind is 19%. Solar is just 16%. Gas is 16%, and bioenergy 14%.

A 100 MW solar plant, on a 16% capacity factor, generates about 140 million units a year. A 100 MW coal plant at 69% generates about 605 million units. To match the annual output of that single coal plant, you would need roughly 4.3 times the solar capacity, over 430 MW of panels. And you would still need to handle nights and cloudy days.

So when you see that solar accounts for 21% of India's installed capacity but only around 7% of actual generation, that gap is not a policy failure. It is physics. A megawatt of solar simply produces far fewer kilowatt-hours in a year than a megawatt of coal or nuclear. This is the reality that means India must install enormous amounts of solar to make a dent in coal's dominance.

## What are the three different ways India's states generate their power?

Open a map of India's power generation, and you see three distinct grids. Not political boundaries, but energy geologies.

First, the hydro states. In the Himalayan north and the northeastern hill states, steep rivers have been harnessed for decades. Himachal Pradesh generates almost 100% of its electricity from hydro. These states did not go clean through recent policies; they were born clean, because the mountains left them no coal and plenty of water. Their carbon intensity sits near the 24 gCO2/kWh floor, but it is not a transition, it is an inheritance.

Second, the wind and solar belt. Rajasthan, Gujarat, and Tamil Nadu have built their way to high clean shares with new capital, not old rivers. Wind corridors in the south and west, and vast solar parks in the desert, have pushed the share of renewables in generation to levels the coal belt can only dream of. These states are actually moving the needle.

Third, the coal heartland. East of the Ganges and across the central belt, coal is king. Chhattisgarh runs on 98% coal, Bihar and Jharkhand on 99% fossil fuels. These states host India's largest coal reserves and generate the power that feeds the national grid, but their own grids remain overwhelmingly dirty. A high clean share in the national average papers over this fundamental divide.

## Where did India actually build all its wind and solar?

Not everywhere. Look at the bars of installed wind and solar capacity in 2024, and the concentration is stark. Rajasthan leads with about 31,700 MW. Gujarat follows with about 29,300 MW. Tamil Nadu about 20,900 MW, Karnataka about 15,700 MW, Maharashtra about 14,200 MW. After that, the numbers drop sharply: Andhra Pradesh about 8,800 MW, Madhya Pradesh about 7,800 MW, Telangana about 5,000 MW, Uttar Pradesh about 3,300 MW, and Haryana about 2,000 MW.

The top five states hold the overwhelming majority of India's renewable hardware. This is the real transition landscape, stripped of the inherited hydro that distorts the clean share map. But again, capacity is not generation. Those roughly 31,700 MW in Rajasthan, with a typical solar capacity factor of around 16% in the desert, translate to far fewer actual units than the installed figure suggests. Still, it is in these states that the physical assets of the energy transition are going into the ground.

## Of the new power added since 2019, how much was clean?

This is the toughest test of a transition. When demand surged, did a state reach for more coal or more clean power? Between 2019 and 2024, India's total generation rose by about 425 TWh. Nationally, only about 26% of that new power came from clean sources. Three-quarters of the growth was fossil.

The state-by-state breakdown is even more revealing. Gujarat is an outlier: it met more than all its new demand with clean power, reducing fossil generation in absolute terms. Its clean share of growth was 117%. Rajasthan (69%) and Tamil Nadu (61%) also covered most of their growth with renewables. But many other states barely moved. Bihar met essentially 0% of its new demand with clean power. Odisha and West Bengal around 1%, Chhattisgarh 4%, Uttar Pradesh 5%. Even Karnataka, with its large renewable fleet, only managed 27% clean growth.

A state can have a high clean share of total generation and still lock in fossil growth at the margin. The marginal test reveals who is truly transitioning and who is just running on inherited hydro or a large but static renewable base.

## If the grid is getting cleaner, why are power emissions still rising in most states?

Because absolute emissions follow the tonnes of coal burned, not the share of clean power. Between 2019 and 2024, India's power-sector CO2 emissions rose by roughly 267 million tonnes. The map of emissions change is almost uniformly red. Chhattisgarh added about 42 million tonnes, Uttar Pradesh about 33, Madhya Pradesh about 26, Odisha about 23, and Bihar about 21. These are massive increases.

Only Gujarat managed a meaningful cut, reducing its power emissions by about 2.4 million tonnes over the same period, a direct consequence of its aggressive solar and wind build. A handful of other states eked out tiny declines, but the overwhelming message is that generation growth is swamping the carbon intensity improvements. India is producing more electricity each year, and the extra power is still largely coal.

Falling carbon intensity per kilowatt-hour is not the same as falling total emissions. As long as the pie keeps growing, the tonnes can rise even as each unit gets marginally cleaner. This is the central honesty the clean share narrative must hold.

## Where do India's power-sector emissions actually come from?

Intensity tells you how dirty each unit is; this chart tells you where the physical tonnes are. In 2024, the largest absolute emitter was Chhattisgarh, at about 135 million tonnes of CO2. Uttar Pradesh was almost identical, about 132 million tonnes, followed by Madhya Pradesh at about 121 million tonnes and Maharashtra at about 113 million tonnes. Rounding out the top ten: West Bengal about 76 million tonnes, Gujarat about 76 million tonnes, Tamil Nadu about 64 million tonnes, Andhra Pradesh about 59 million tonnes, Odisha about 56 million tonnes, and Rajasthan about 56 million tonnes.

Notice that some of these are not the states with the highest carbon intensity. Chhattisgarh and Uttar Pradesh are both coal-heavy, but Maharashtra and Gujarat are not the worst in intensity terms. Yet they appear high on the absolute list because they generate a lot of electricity. This is where decarbonisation efforts must focus: not only on making each unit cleaner, but on displacing the sheer volume of coal generation in these big states.

Again, these are production-side emissions. A state that hosts a large coal power complex may be generating electricity and emissions for consumers hundreds of kilometres away, so the climate burden is physically located there but economically shared.

## How does the monsoon decide India's clean power output?

India's clean power is not a flat, steady stream. It breathes with the seasons, and the monsoon is its lungs. Using monthly data for 2024, we can see the rhythm.

Wind generation nearly quadruples from its November low to its July high, a 4.4-fold swing. Hydro more than triples from its February trough to its August-September peak, a 3.6-fold swing. Solar is the steadiest, with only about a 1.4-fold swing between its winter low and summer high, but even that matters.

Coal, the workhorse, dips noticeably during the monsoon months as hydro and wind surge. In May 2024, coal generated about 123 TWh; by September, that had fallen to about 100 TWh. The monsoon effectively pushes coal aside for a few months, giving the grid a cleaner season. But this is one year's data; it shows a seasonal shape, not a long-term trend. Dry years will suppress hydro, and the wind does not always blow. The clean grid's output is hostage to the sky.

This variability is the central challenge. India must manage a grid where clean power can surge 3-4 times between months, and where the backup, still coal, has to be ready to ramp up when the rains end.

## How should you read these numbers?

None of these numbers are magic. They come with clear boundaries and a few honest footnotes.

The state-level data is drawn from Ember's India electricity dataset, which compiles figures from the Central Electricity Authority (CEA) and the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) and releases them under a Creative Commons licence. The national long-run series from 2000 to 2025 comes from Ember's separately built electricity database. These two Ember series are compiled differently and differ a little in level: the national series puts 2024 coal generation somewhat higher than the state figures add up to, so the long-run national chart and the state-level charts are best read on their own terms rather than summed against each other. The state data covers only the years 2019 to 2024. That is a six-year snapshot of the current transition, not a multi-decade trend. Longer comparisons at the state level are not yet possible with this dataset.

Every figure in these charts is electricity generated inside a state's boundaries. It is not the electricity consumed by that state's residents. Power crosses borders constantly, so a state with a clean generation mix may still use dirty power imported from elsewhere, and a coal-heavy state may be exporting its emissions.

The carbon intensity floor of about 24 gCO2/kWh seen in hydro-dominated states is a methodology constant, not a measured value. It should not be over-interpreted as a precise zero-carbon claim. Similarly, the emissions figures are estimates based on generation and fuel types; they are not direct stack measurements.

Finally, correlation is not causation. When a state's clean share rises as coal emissions also rise, that does not mean the clean additions caused the coal growth. It reflects the deeper reality that India's electricity demand is growing so fast that it is adding all sources simultaneously. The numbers in these charts reveal what is happening, not always why.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: India's electricity transition is not one story but about thirty, with state grids ranging from near-zero-carbon to almost pure coal. The national average masks a lopsided reality where a few states race ahead on new solar and wind while the coal belt stays locked, and even as the clean share rises, coal generation keeps growing.

selectedDataPoints.label: Clean share of India's electricity, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: shows the national progress to over a quarter clean

selectedDataPoints.label: Clean share of India's electricity, earliest
selectedDataPoints.reason: baseline to measure the climb from 17%

selectedDataPoints.label: Coal generation, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: reveals the absolute scale of coal growth

selectedDataPoints.label: Coal generation, earliest
Solar generation, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: highlight solar's steep rise to around 196 TWh

selectedDataPoints.label: How much of the year each power source actually runs: value
Where India built its wind and solar: value
Of the new power added since 2019, how much was clean?: value
selectedDataPoints.reason: the decisive marginal test of transition

selectedDataPoints.label: Where India's power-sector emissions actually come from: value
selectedDataPoints.reason: anchors absolute emissions where decarbonisation is needed

pullQuotes.quote: Coal generation nearly quadrupled from 2000 to 2024, even as the clean share rose from 17% to 27%.
Only about a quarter of the new electricity India generated since 2019 came from clean sources.
pullQuotes.numberLabel: Clean share of growth national estimate

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Grams of carbon dioxide emitted for each kilowatt-hour of electricity generated. It measures how dirty a unit of electricity is.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The key metric on the first map; a grid with 800 gCO2/kWh is mostly coal, while one near zero is hydro or wind.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Terawatt-hour. One TWh is a billion kilowatt-hours, roughly the annual electricity use of a million Indian homes.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: All generation figures are in TWh; helps compare the scale of coal, solar, and total demand.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The share of the year a power plant actually runs at full capacity. A solar panel might have a 16% capacity factor because the sun doesn't shine at night.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Explains why solar's installed capacity share (21%) far exceeds its generation share (7%).

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The maximum power a plant can produce at once, measured in megawatts (MW). It's like the size of a pipe, not the water that flows through it.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: States with high installed solar capacity may still generate modest amounts because of low capacity factors.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: How much CO2 is released per unit of electricity generated, usually gCO2/kWh. A low number means cleaner power.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The main way we compare how clean different states' electricity really is.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The percentage of total electricity generation that comes from renewables (hydro, wind, solar, biomass) plus nuclear.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The headline metric for how much of India's grid is low-carbon. But it can rise even as coal generation grows.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The combination of fuels used to produce electricity: coal, gas, hydro, nuclear, solar, wind, etc.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Different states have very different mixes, which explains why some are clean and others are coal-heavy.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Coal, natural gas, and oil. Burning them for electricity releases CO2, the main driver of climate change.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Almost all India's power emissions come from burning coal; gas plays a minor role.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Energy sources that replenish naturally: solar, wind, hydro, and biomass. They produce little or no CO2 when generating electricity.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The growth of renewables, especially solar, is the main reason India's clean share is rising.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Electricity produced by splitting atoms in a reactor. It is low-carbon but not renewable, and it runs steadily.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Nuclear provides about 3% of India's electricity and has a high capacity factor (76%), so it punches above its installed capacity.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The seasonal rainy season in South Asia (June, September). It fills rivers for hydro and drives strong winds.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Explains why hydro and wind output surge in the second half of the year, giving India's clean power a strong seasonal rhythm.

visualizationDecisions.reason: A map is the clearest way to show the spread across states; one year snapshot fits the state data vintage.
A line chart shows the long-run trend clearly, and the full window reveals the rise from ~17% to ~27%.
A multi-line chart allows direct comparison of absolute generation by fuel over the same period.
Horizontal bars make comparing capacity factors across fuels easy on a phone screen.
Three small maps or panes quickly convey the dominant source in each region.
Bars rank states by installed capacity cleanly; important to note capacity factor separately.
Bars show the clean fraction of growth, easy to scan for leaders and laggards.
A red/blue map quickly highlights where emissions rose and where they (rarely) fell.
Bars rank absolute emitters and show the concentration in a few large coal-burning states.

chartExplainers.title: How clean is each state's electricity?
chartExplainers.takeaway: Carbon intensity of state power generation runs from near zero in Himalayan hydro states to over 800 gCO2/kWh in the coal belt, there is no single Indian grid.
chartExplainers.detail: This choropleth shades each state by the grams of CO2 emitted per kilowatt-hour of electricity generated inside its borders in 2024. The dark red core, Bihar, Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, sits above 800 gCO2/kWh, almost entirely powered by coal. The pale blue Himalayan arc and northeast are near the methodology floor of 24 gCO2/kWh, reflecting hydro dominance. The national average, about 620, is the midpoint of an enormous spread. Remember, this is where power is made, not where it is used; a coal-heavy state may be exporting electricity to a cleaner-consuming state.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The whole argument in one map: there is no single Indian grid, there are thirty, ranging from near-zero-carbon to almost pure coal.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Darker red means dirtier electricity per unit; lighter blue means cleaner.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Assuming a clean state means its residents use clean electricity; power crosses state lines, so this is generation, not consumption.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A choropleth is scannable if labels are clear and colour ramp is intuitive.

chartExplainers.title: Nationally, the grid really is getting cleaner
chartExplainers.takeaway: The clean share of India's electricity (renewables + nuclear) climbed from 16.8% in 2000 to 26.7% in 2025, a genuine improvement.
chartExplainers.detail: The line plots the clean share of total annual generation over a quarter-century. The rise is not smooth, a spike in 2020 reflects pandemic demand suppression, but the trend is unmistakable. Clean here includes large hydro, wind, solar, biomass, and nuclear. At over a quarter, one in four units now come from low-carbon sources. However, a rising share can coexist with rising coal generation if total demand grows fast enough.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Start with the true national headline before complicating it: the clean share has climbed, and that is real progress.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The upward slope shows clean sources gaining ground; the percentage on the y-axis is the fraction of all electricity generated.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Reading the clean share as proof that coal is falling; it can rise even as coal grows if total generation grows faster.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A single line chart with annual points works well on a narrow screen.

chartExplainers.title: But coal kept growing the whole time
chartExplainers.takeaway: Coal generation nearly quadrupled from 2000 to 2025, while solar surged from near zero but remains dwarfed by coal.
chartExplainers.detail: Six lines show absolute generation (TWh) by source. Coal dominates utterly, rising from roughly 390 to nearly 1,500 TWh. Solar is the steepest climber, from near zero to about 196 TWh, but is still far below coal. Wind rose to about 104 TWh, hydro to about 178 TWh, nuclear to about 54 TWh, and gas fell slightly to about 49 TWh. The simultaneous rise of clean shares and coal generation is not a contradiction; it reflects a pie expanding so fast that both slices grew. India needed more power, and renewable additions could not yet meet the full increment.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The honest twist: a rising clean share hides the fact that coal generation almost quadrupled, because demand grew faster than clean supply.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the heights of the lines at each year; coal's line dwarfs all others. Solar is tiny but growing fast.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Focusing only on the clean share line while ignoring the absolute coal line; both are necessary.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A multi-line chart can be crowded; use distinct colours and a legend that stacks cleanly.

chartExplainers.title: Why a megawatt of solar isn't a megawatt of coal
chartExplainers.takeaway: Solar runs only 16% of the year, while coal runs 69%, so a solar plant needs over four times the capacity to match a coal plant's annual output.
chartExplainers.detail: Horizontal bars show the capacity factor for each fuel in India in 2024. Nuclear tops at 76%, coal 69%, hydro 34%, wind 19%, solar and gas both 16%, and bioenergy 14%. A 100 MW solar plant at 16% produces about 140 million units a year; a 100 MW coal plant at 69% produces about 605 million. This explains why solar's high installed capacity share (21%) translates to only about 7% of generation. Intermittency is not a design failing, it is physics. A low capacity factor is the reason India must install massive amounts of solar to meaningfully displace coal.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The hidden reason capacity and generation tell different stories: intermittent sources run only a fraction of the time.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Longer bars mean the fuel runs more hours at full power. Compare the gap between nuclear/coal and solar/wind.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Equating installed capacity with expected generation; a 100 MW solar plant produces far fewer units annually than a 100 MW coal plant.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars are easy to scan from top to bottom on a phone.

chartExplainers.title: Three different ways to run a power grid
chartExplainers.takeaway: Hydro dominates the Himalayan north, wind and solar the west and south, and coal the eastern and Gangetic plain, a clean grid can be inherited or built.
chartExplainers.detail: Three small maps or panes each shade states by the share of generation from one source: hydro, wind+solar, and coal. The hydro map lights up Himachal, Uttarakhand, Sikkim, and the northeast, states that were always clean because of rivers. The wind+solar map highlights Rajasthan, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, states that built their clean share with new capital. The coal map shows Chhattisgarh (98% coal), Bihar and Jharkhand (99% fossil), and the central plain. A high clean share is not the same as leading the energy transition; it can simply be geography.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Why states diverge so sharply: a clean grid can mean inherited Himalayan hydro or newly-built wind and solar, completely different maps.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at each map separately: the darkest shades reveal the dominant source in that region.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Assuming all high-clean-share states are recent success stories; some were always clean because of geography.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Small multiples maps work if they are simple and legends are clear; use distinct colour scales.

chartExplainers.title: Where India actually built its wind and solar
chartExplainers.takeaway: Just five states, Rajasthan, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Maharashtra, hold the vast majority of installed wind and solar capacity.
chartExplainers.detail: Horizontal bars rank states by combined wind and solar installed capacity in megawatts as of 2024. Rajasthan tops at about 31,700 MW, Gujarat about 29,300, Tamil Nadu about 20,900, Karnataka about 15,700, Maharashtra about 14,200. Andhra Pradesh (about 8,800), Madhya Pradesh (about 7,800), and others trail far behind. This concentration shows the real transition geography: a handful of states are bearing the hardware build-out. But installed capacity is not generation, solar panels at 16% capacity factor mean those roughly 31,700 MW produce far fewer annual units than a similar megawattage of coal.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Strip away the inherited hydro and this is the real transition: a handful of states are doing most of the building.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Longer bars mean more megawatts of panels and turbines on the ground in that state.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Assuming installed capacity directly translates to generation share; it overstates solar and wind's contribution to the electricity mix.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars rank cleanly and avoid truncation on narrow screens.

chartExplainers.title: Of the new power added since 2019, how much was clean?
chartExplainers.takeaway: Nationally, only about 26% of new generation was clean; Gujarat met over 100% of its growth with clean power, while Bihar met essentially none.
chartExplainers.detail: Bars show the share of each state's generation growth between 2019 and 2024 that came from clean sources (renewables + nuclear). Gujarat is an outlier at 117%, meaning it cut fossil generation while meeting new demand. Rajasthan (69%) and Tamil Nadu (61%) also covered most growth cleanly. But many states barely moved: Bihar at essentially 0%, Odisha and West Bengal around 1%, Chhattisgarh 4%, Uttar Pradesh 5%. This marginal test reveals who is actually transitioning versus simply maintaining a high clean share from hydro or static renewables.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The most decisive test of a transition: when a state needed more electricity, did it reach for clean or for coal?
chartExplainers.howToRead: Bars showing 100% or above mean fossil generation fell; near 0% means almost all new power was fossil.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Using a state's existing clean share to judge its recent action; a state can be clean overall yet meet new demand with coal.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars make state ranking clear; annotate outliers to draw attention.

chartExplainers.title: Power emissions are still rising across most of India
chartExplainers.takeaway: Absolute power-sector CO2 rose by roughly 267 million tonnes from 2019 to 2024, with only Gujarat cutting meaningfully.
chartExplainers.detail: A choropleth shades states by the change in absolute CO2 emissions from power generation between 2019 and 2024. Red marks increases, and the map is overwhelmingly red. Chhattisgarh added about 42 million tonnes, Uttar Pradesh about 33, Madhya Pradesh about 26, Odisha about 23, Bihar about 21. Gujarat is a rare blue spot, reducing emissions by about 2.4 million tonnes thanks to its solar and wind surge. Falling carbon intensity per unit is not falling total emissions; India's generation growth swamps efficiency gains. This map shows where the climate pressure is mounting.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The sobering counterpoint: in absolute tonnes, India's power emissions are still going up almost everywhere.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Redder states added more CO2; blue means a decline. Gujarat's blue is nearly unique.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Inferring that because carbon intensity per unit is falling, total emissions must be falling; generation growth can easily outpace intensity gains.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A red-green colour scale needs clear labelling for colour-blind readers; a divergent blue-red may work better.

chartExplainers.title: Where India's power emissions actually come from
chartExplainers.takeaway: Chhattisgarh emits the most absolute CO2, at about 135 million tonnes, followed closely by Uttar Pradesh and Madhya Pradesh, these are the states where decarbonisation must focus.
chartExplainers.detail: Horizontal bars show absolute power-sector CO2 emissions in 2024. Chhattisgarh tops at about 135 million tonnes, Uttar Pradesh about 132 million, Madhya Pradesh about 121 million, Maharashtra about 113 million, West Bengal about 76 million, Gujarat about 76 million, Tamil Nadu about 64 million, Andhra Pradesh about 59 million, Odisha about 56 million, Rajasthan about 56 million. Several of these are not the highest intensity states, but they generate the most power. Decarbonising India's grid means reducing these absolute tonnes, not just improving the carbon intensity of each unit. Again, these are production-side emissions; a coal export state may carry the burden for consumption elsewhere.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Intensity tells you how dirty each unit is; this tells you where the tonnes physically come from, which is where decarbonisation has to happen.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Bigger bars mean more total CO2 from power generation in that state.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Confusing total emissions with emission intensity; a state can be a top emitter simply because it generates a lot of power, not because it is inefficient.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars rank top emitters clearly; use large numbers for kilotonnes.

chartExplainers.title: The clean grid runs on the monsoon's calendar
chartExplainers.takeaway: Wind generation nearly quadruples from November to July, hydro more than triples from February to September, while solar is steadier, all in a single year's rhythm.
chartExplainers.detail: Three lines show monthly generation (GWh) for hydro, wind, and solar in India during 2024. Wind swings 4.4-fold between its November trough and July peak. Hydro swings 3.6-fold from February to August-September. Solar varies only about 1.4-fold. The charts reveal the monsoon's imprint: as rivers swell and winds strengthen, renewable output surges, and coal generation dips from about 123 TWh in May to about 100 TWh in September. This seasonal shape is not a trend, it is one year, but it illustrates the variability the grid must manage, and why storage and flexible coal plants matter.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Renewables are not steady: India's clean power has a strong seasonal rhythm that the rest of the grid has to work around.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Hydro and wind lines peak in the second half of the year; solar peaks in spring. Watch how they move together.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Extrapolating a monthly peak to an annual average; this is one year's seasonal shape, not a trend.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Three lines can be legible if colours are distinct and axes are labelled clearly.

sectionVisualMap.heading: How clean is each state's electricity?
Has India's electricity grid really been getting cleaner nationally?
If the grid is getting cleaner, why did coal generation keep growing?
Why doesn't a megawatt of solar replace a megawatt of coal?
What are the three different ways India's states generate their power?
Where did India actually build all its wind and solar?
Of the new power added since 2019, how much was clean?
If the grid is getting cleaner, why are power emissions still rising in most states?
Where do India's power-sector emissions actually come from?
How does the monsoon decide India's clean power output?
How should you read these numbers?

sourceNotes: State-level data from Ember's India electricity dataset, compiled from Central Electricity Authority (CEA) and Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), released under Creative Commons license.
National long-run series (2000-2025) from Ember's separately built electricity database.
caveats: State-level data covers only 2019-2024, a six-year snapshot, not a long-term trend.
All figures are electricity generated inside a state, not consumed there; power trade between states is common.
The carbon intensity floor of ~24 gCO2/kWh in clean-dominated grids is a methodology constant, not a measured zero.
Absolute emissions are estimated from generation and fuel types, not direct stack measurements.
Hydro output varies with monsoon; a single year can misrepresent a state's typical clean share.
Correlation does not imply causation; multiple factors move together.
Capacity factors are national averages and vary by location and technology.
lockedNumbersUsed: Clean share of India's electricity, latest
Clean share of India's electricity, earliest
How much of the year each power source actually runs: value
Where India built its wind and solar: value
Of the new power added since 2019, how much was clean?: value
Where India's power-sector emissions actually come from: value

editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: India's electricity is getting cleaner nationally, but the real story is that about thirty state grids are on wildly different paths: some are racing ahead on solar and wind while others burn nearly pure coal, and underneath a rising clean share, coal generation still grew to nearly 1,500 TWh as demand outpaced clean supply.
requiredConcepts: The framing fact and the spine of the whole article: India does not have one electricity grid getting cleaner at one pace, it has about thirty state grids that range from almost zero-carbon to almost pure coal. In 2024 the carbon intensity of state power generation runs from about 24 gCO2/kWh in the hydro-rich Himalayan and northeastern states (Himachal Pradesh, Jammu and Kashmir, Sikkim, Arunachal Pradesh and others) to over 800 in the coal belt (Bihar and Jharkhand about 815, Chhattisgarh about 806). The national average is about 620 gCO2/kWh. The national number is an average that hides this entire spread

The true national headline, which must be stated before it is complicated: the clean share of India's electricity generation (renewables plus nuclear) has climbed from about 17% in 2000 to over a quarter (about 27%) by the mid-2020s. The grid genuinely is getting cleaner in this sense. Clean here means renewables plus nuclear, not renewables alone

The honest twist that is the heart of the article: a rising clean SHARE does not mean falling coal. In absolute terms India's coal generation almost quadrupled over the same period, from roughly 390 TWh in 2000 to nearly 1,500 TWh in 2024, because total electricity demand grew faster than clean supply could be added. Solar generation rose from essentially zero to roughly 200 TWh, real and steep, but still small next to coal. Both facts are true at once: the clean slice of the pie grew while the coal slice grew too, because the whole pie grew. Hold them together

Capacity is not generation, and the gap is the point: in 2024 solar was about 21% of India's installed capacity but only about 7% of the electricity actually generated, and wind about 10% of capacity for about 4.5% of generation, while coal was about 47% of capacity but about 74% of generation. The reason is the capacity factor, the share of the year a source runs at full output: roughly 76% for nuclear and 69% for coal, against about 34% for hydro, 19% for wind and only 16% for solar. A low capacity factor is physics, not failure; it means India must install far more solar to match the yearly output of a coal plant. Present these as approximate figures

The crucial distinction that explains why states diverge, and the single most important honesty point: a clean grid can mean two completely different things. Some states were born clean on inherited large hydro decided by geography (Himachal Pradesh generates almost entirely from hydro, around 100%), which is not a transition anyone is running. Other states built their way clean with new wind and solar (Rajasthan, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu). A high clean share is therefore NOT the same as leading the energy transition, and the article must never conflate the two

The decisive marginal test, which is the strongest single finding in the piece: of all the extra electricity India generated between 2019 and 2024 (generation rose about 31%, from roughly 1,380 to 1,800 TWh), only about a quarter (around 26%) came from clean sources; roughly three-quarters of the new power was fossil. By state the spread is stark: Gujarat met more than all its new demand with clean power (it actually cut fossil generation), Rajasthan about 69% and Tamil Nadu about 61%, while Bihar met essentially 0%, Odisha and West Bengal about 1%, Chhattisgarh about 4% and Uttar Pradesh about 5% of their growth with clean power. A high clean share of TOTAL generation can hide a dirty MARGIN of new growth

The hard counterpoint on emissions, which the article must not soften: in absolute tonnes India's power-sector CO2 is still rising almost everywhere. National power emissions rose by roughly 267 million tonnes between 2019 and 2024. The biggest increases were Chhattisgarh (about +42 million tonnes), Uttar Pradesh (about +33), Madhya Pradesh (about +26), Odisha (about +23) and Bihar (about +21); Gujarat was almost the only state to cut its power emissions meaningfully (about -2.4 million tonnes). Falling carbon intensity per unit does not mean falling total emissions, because generation is growing so fast

The seasonal rhythm, from 2024 monthly data: India's renewable output swings hard with the monsoon. Wind generation nearly quadruples from its November low to its July peak (about a 4.4-fold swing), hydro more than triples from its February low to its August-September peak (about a 3.6-fold swing), while solar is comparatively steady (about a 1.4-fold swing across the year). Coal generation dips during the monsoon months (it falls from about 123 TWh in May to about 100 TWh in September) as hydro and wind fill in. This is one year, so it shows a seasonal shape, not a trend

The three-Indias geography, to be described from the maps: hydro dominates the Himalayan north and the northeast; wind and solar dominate the western desert (Rajasthan, Gujarat) and the south (Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh); coal dominates the east and the Gangetic plain (Chhattisgarh about 98% coal, Bihar and Jharkhand about 99% fossil, West Bengal, Uttar Pradesh, Odisha)

Where the wind and solar were actually built, by installed capacity in 2024 (in megawatts): Rajasthan leads at about 31,700 MW, Gujarat about 29,300, Tamil Nadu about 20,900, Karnataka about 15,700, Maharashtra about 14,200. A handful of states are doing most of the building. Be clear that installed capacity is not generation: solar and wind are intermittent, so a megawatt of solar produces far fewer units over a year than a megawatt of coal

Where the power-sector emissions physically come from, in absolute tonnes in 2024 (this is mass, a different question from intensity): Chhattisgarh emits the most at roughly 135 million tonnes of CO2, followed by Uttar Pradesh about 132 and Madhya Pradesh about 121. Intensity tells you how dirty each unit is; absolute emissions tell you where the tonnes are, which is where decarbonisation has to happen. A big coal-burning state can top the absolute list without having the single highest intensity

Who actually moved between 2019 and 2024, measured as the change in clean share of generation in percentage points: Rajasthan rose the most (about +17 points) and Gujarat also jumped, on new solar and wind. Crucially, being clean today and getting cleaner are different things, and some states went backwards: Karnataka's clean share FELL by about 10 points over these years. Present this honestly, including the backsliders

The hydro-volatility caveat, essential for reading the 2019-2024 change honestly: states that lean on hydro swing with the monsoon. A dry year cuts hydro output and the clean share can fall even if the state burnt no extra coal. So a single-year change can mislead, and Karnataka's apparent slide partly reflects rainfall and fast-growing demand, not a deliberate turn back to coal. Read multi-year direction, not one endpoint gap

The consumption-versus-generation caveat, which must be stated wherever per-state figures appear: every number here is electricity GENERATED inside a state, not the electricity its residents consume. Power crosses state lines constantly, so a state can generate a lot of coal power that is used elsewhere, and a low-generating state is not necessarily a low-consuming one. This is a map of where power is made, not where it is used

VINTAGE DISCIPLINE: the state-level data covers calendar years 2019 to 2024 only, a six-year snapshot of the current transition, not a long historical trend; the national long-arc series runs from 2000 and extends to about 2025. Always keep the difference clear and never imply the state data shows a multi-decade trend

NUMBER DISCIPLINE is absolute: every statistic in the prose must come from the evidence packet's locked numbers or the specific figures stated in these concepts; never invent, recall or estimate a number. Present figures as rounded approximations ('about 27%', 'nearly 1,500 TWh', 'roughly 31,700 MW'), never false-precision decimals. The clean-grid carbon-intensity floor of about 24 gCO2/kWh is a methodology constant applied to clean-dominated grids, not a measured value, and should not be over-read. Anchor every number when it first appears

Keep the macha layer warm and plain-spoken, a sharp friend explaining what India's lopsided electricity transition really means: the country is not cleaning up its grid evenly, it is running thirty different experiments at once, and the averages flatter the laggards while hiding the leaders. The playfulness lives in the headings; the body stays grounded and genuinely helpful, and it summarises what the whole page means, not one chart

sectionPlan.heading: How clean is each state's electricity?
sectionPlan.job: Introduce the central frame: India has not one grid but about thirty, with carbon intensity ranging from near zero to over 800 gCO2/kWh. Show that the national average of about 620 gCO2/kWh hides extreme spread, from Himachal Pradesh’s near-zero hydro to Bihar’s coal-heavy grid.
conceptsToExplain: carbon intensity (gCO2/kWh)
difference between generation and consumption of electricity
why hydro states are low without having 'transitioned'
sectionPlan.caveat: The carbon intensity floor of ~24 gCO2/kWh is a methodology constant, not a measured zero. Also, this is electricity generated in the state, not consumed there.

sectionPlan.heading: Has India's electricity grid really been getting cleaner nationally?
sectionPlan.job: Present the true national headline: the clean share (renewables + nuclear) has climbed from about 17% in 2000 to over 27% by 2025. Acknowledge the real progress before complicating it, explaining what ‘clean’ includes.
lockedNumbersToUse: Clean share of India's electricity, latest
Clean share of India's electricity, earliest
conceptsToExplain: clean share (renewables + nuclear)
generation mix
TWh as a unit of electricity
sectionPlan.caveat: Clean share alone does not tell you whether coal is falling; it is a ratio that can rise even as coal grows.

sectionPlan.heading: If the grid is getting cleaner, why did coal generation keep growing?
sectionPlan.job: Show the honest twist: a rising clean share hides the fact that coal generation almost quadrupled from about 390 TWh to nearly 1,500 TWh because total electricity demand grew faster than clean supply. Solar surged from essentially zero to roughly 200 TWh, but coal dominates.
conceptsToExplain: absolute generation (TWh)
the pie growing: how both clean and fossil slices can expand
why a rising clean share does not equal falling coal
sectionPlan.caveat: Gas generation actually fell slightly, but coal did all the heavy lifting for fossil growth.

sectionPlan.heading: Why a megawatt of solar isn't a megawatt of coal
sectionPlan.job: Explain that installed capacity is not generation. Use capacity factors: coal runs ~69% of the year, solar only ~16%, so a solar plant needs far more capacity to match a coal plant's annual output. This physics is why high solar capacity share doesn't mean high generation share.
lockedNumbersToUse: How much of the year each power source actually runs: value
conceptsToExplain: capacity factor
installed capacity vs. generation
intermittency and why it's not a design failure
sectionPlan.caveat: Capacity factors are approximate averages; solar in Rajasthan can differ from solar in Kerala, but the national 16% figure makes the point.

sectionPlan.heading: What are the three different ways India's states generate their power?
sectionPlan.job: Show the three-distinct geographies of power generation: hydro-dominated Himalayan states, wind-solar-rich western and southern states, and coal-dominated eastern states. Emphasise that a clean grid can be inherited (hydro) or built (solar/wind), and this explains the divergence.
conceptsToExplain: generation mix (share of each source)
hydro as an inherited clean source
wind and solar as built clean sources
why high clean share is not the same as leading the energy transition
sectionPlan.caveat: The generation mix is for 2024 only; hydro output varies with monsoon, so a single year may over- or understate a state’s typical clean share.

sectionPlan.heading: Where did India actually build all its wind and solar?
sectionPlan.job: Highlight the concentration of installed wind and solar capacity: Rajasthan (~31,700 MW), Gujarat (~29,300), Tamil Nadu (~20,900), Karnataka (~15,700), Maharashtra (~14,200). Show that a handful of states are doing most of the building, and note that capacity is not generation.
lockedNumbersToUse: Where India built its wind and solar: value
conceptsToExplain: installed capacity (MW)
capacity factor, again
solar and wind are intermittent, so more capacity is needed for the same annual output
sectionPlan.caveat: Installed capacity numbers can inflate expectations; always ask ‘how much does this actually generate in a year?’

sectionPlan.heading: Of the new power added since 2019, how much was clean?
sectionPlan.job: Apply the marginal test: of the ~420 TWh of new generation since 2019, only about 26% came from clean sources. Show state variation: Gujarat met more than 100% of new demand with clean power (cut fossil), while Bihar met essentially 0%, Odisha 1%, etc.
lockedNumbersToUse: Of the new power added since 2019, how much was clean?: value
conceptsToExplain: the difference between clean share of total vs. clean share of growth
why a high existing clean share can hide a dirty growth margin
sectionPlan.caveat: States with high hydro shares can see their clean share fluctuate due to monsoon, not just policy. A single six-year window may miss longer trends.

sectionPlan.heading: If the grid is getting cleaner, why are power emissions still rising in most states?
sectionPlan.job: Show the hard counterpoint: absolute power-sector CO2 emissions are still climbing almost everywhere. Between 2019 and 2024, national power emissions rose by about 267 million tonnes. Chhattisgarh added ~42 Mt, Uttar Pradesh ~33 Mt, Madhya Pradesh ~26 Mt. Falling carbon intensity does not mean falling total emissions.
conceptsToExplain: carbon intensity vs. absolute emissions
how fast-rising generation swamps efficiency gains
sectionPlan.caveat: These are emissions from generation inside the state, not consumption-based emissions. Power exports can shift the apparent burden.

sectionPlan.heading: Where do India's power-sector emissions actually come from?
sectionPlan.job: Anchor the absolute mass of emissions in 2024: Chhattisgarh tops at ~135 MtCO2, followed closely by Uttar Pradesh (~132 Mt) and Madhya Pradesh (~121 Mt). Intensity tells you how dirty each unit is; raw tonnes tell you where decarbonisation must focus.
lockedNumbersToUse: Where India's power-sector emissions actually come from: value
conceptsToExplain: absolute emissions (mass)
why a state can be top emitter without highest intensity
sectionPlan.caveat: Again, generation vs. consumption. A coal-exporting state may appear as a large emitter even if its own residents use less power.

sectionPlan.heading: How does the monsoon decide India's clean power output?
sectionPlan.job: Illustrate the seasonal rhythm of renewables using 2024 monthly data: wind nearly quadruples from November to July, hydro more than triples from February to August-September, solar is steadier but still swings ~1.4x. Coal dips during monsoon months as hydro and wind fill in, falling from ~123 TWh in May to ~100 TWh in September.
conceptsToExplain: seasonal variation
why monsoon drives hydro and wind
solar’s relative steadiness
sectionPlan.caveat: This is one year of monthly data; it shows a typical seasonal shape, not a long-term trend.

sectionPlan.heading: How should you read these numbers?
sectionPlan.job: Provide a short, honest methodology paragraph. Name sources: Ember’s state-level India electricity data, compiled from Central Electricity Authority (CEA) and Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), under Creative Commons; and Ember’s national electricity series for 2000-2024. State the key caveats: state-level data covers 2019-2024 only, so a snapshot; figures are generation inside the state, not consumption; the ~24 gCO2/kWh floor is a methodology constant; correlation is not causation; and the national average hides enormous spread.
conceptsToExplain: generation vs. consumption
data vintage and limitations
methodology constant for carbon intensity floor

chartPlan.title: How clean is each state's electricity?
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The whole argument in one map: there is no single Indian grid, there are thirty, ranging from near-zero-carbon to almost pure coal.
chartPlan.howToRead: Colors represent gCO2 emitted per kWh generated inside the state. Darker red = dirtier. Compare states, not just the national average.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Assuming a clean state means its residents use clean electricity; power crosses state lines, so this is generation, not consumption.

chartPlan.title: Nationally, the grid really is getting cleaner
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Start with the true national headline: the clean share of electricity has climbed from about 17% to over a quarter, real progress.
chartPlan.howToRead: The rising line shows the clean share (renewables + nuclear) over time. Spike in 2020 partly reflects lockdown demand drop.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Reading the clean share as proof that coal is falling; it can rise even as coal grows if total generation grows faster.

chartPlan.title: But coal kept growing the whole time
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The honest twist: a rising clean share hides the fact that coal generation almost quadrupled, because demand grew faster than clean supply.
chartPlan.howToRead: Each line is absolute generation (TWh) from a fuel. Coal dwarfs all others. Solar grows steeply but from near zero.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Focusing only on the clean share line while ignoring the absolute coal line; both are necessary.

chartPlan.title: Why a megawatt of solar isn't a megawatt of coal
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The hidden reason capacity and generation tell different stories: intermittent sources run only a fraction of the time.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars show % of the year each source actually runs at full capacity. Higher bars mean more reliable output per MW of installed capacity.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Equating installed capacity with expected generation; a 100 MW solar plant produces far fewer units annually than a 100 MW coal plant.

chartPlan.title: Three different ways to run a power grid
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Why states diverge so sharply: a clean grid can mean inherited Himalayan hydro or newly-built wind and solar, completely different maps.
chartPlan.howToRead: Three small maps or panes, each shaded by share of a source: hydro, wind+solar, coal. See which source dominates each region.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Assuming all high-clean-share states are recent success stories; some were always clean because of geography.

chartPlan.title: Where India actually built its wind and solar
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Strip away the inherited hydro and this is the real transition: a handful of states are doing most of the building.
chartPlan.howToRead: Horizontal bars show installed wind+solar capacity (MW) per state. Longer bars mean more hardware on the ground, but remember capacity factor.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Assuming installed capacity directly translates to generation share; it overstates solar and wind’s contribution to the electricity mix.

chartPlan.title: Of the new power added since 2019, how much was clean?
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The most decisive test of a transition: when a state needed more electricity, did it reach for clean or for coal?
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars show % of new generation growth that was clean. Above 100 means fossil generation actually fell; near 0 means almost all new power was fossil.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Using a state’s existing clean share to judge its recent action; a state can be clean overall yet meet new demand with coal.

chartPlan.title: Power emissions are still rising across most of India
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The sobering counterpoint: in absolute tonnes, India’s power emissions are still going up almost everywhere.
chartPlan.howToRead: Map shows change in power-sector CO2 (kt) from 2019 to 2024. Redder = larger rise. Few states show a decline.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Inferring that because carbon intensity per unit is falling, total emissions must be falling; generation growth can easily outpace intensity gains.

chartPlan.title: Where India's power emissions actually come from
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Intensity tells you how dirty each unit is; this tells you where the tonnes physically come from, which is where decarbonisation has to happen.
chartPlan.howToRead: Horizontal bars show absolute CO2 emissions (kt) from power generation in 2024. Bigger bars = bigger climate challenge inside that state.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Confusing total emissions with emission intensity; a state can be a top emitter simply because it generates a lot of power, not because it is inefficient.

chartPlan.title: The clean grid runs on the monsoon's calendar
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Renewables are not steady: India’s clean power has a strong seasonal rhythm that the rest of the grid has to work around.
chartPlan.howToRead: Three lines show monthly generation (GWh) for hydro, wind, solar in 2024. Hydro and wind peak with the monsoon; solar peaks in spring.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Extrapolating a monthly peak to an annual average; this is one year’s seasonal shape, not a trend.

visualizationDecisions.reason: A map is the clearest way to show the spread across states; one year snapshot fits the state data vintage.
A line chart shows the long-run trend clearly, and the full window reveals the rise from ~17% to ~27%.
A multi-line chart allows direct comparison of absolute generation by fuel over the same period.
Horizontal bars make comparing capacity factors across fuels easy on a phone screen.
Three small maps or panes quickly convey the dominant source in each region.
Bars rank states by installed capacity cleanly; important to note capacity factor separately.
Bars show the clean fraction of growth, easy to scan for leaders and laggards.
A red/blue map quickly highlights where emissions rose and where they (rarely) fell.
Bars rank absolute emitters and show the concentration in a few large coal-burning states.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Grams of carbon dioxide emitted for each kilowatt-hour of electricity generated. It measures how dirty a unit of electricity is.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The key metric on the first map; a grid with 800 gCO2/kWh is mostly coal, while one near zero is hydro or wind.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Terawatt-hour. One TWh is a billion kilowatt-hours, roughly the annual electricity use of a million Indian homes.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: All generation figures are in TWh; helps compare the scale of coal, solar, and total demand.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The share of the year a power plant actually runs at full capacity. A solar panel might have a 16% capacity factor because the sun doesn't shine at night.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Explains why solar’s installed capacity share (21%) far exceeds its generation share (7%).

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The maximum power a plant can produce at once, measured in megawatts (MW). It’s like the size of a pipe, not the water that flows through it.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: States with high installed solar capacity may still generate modest amounts because of low capacity factors.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: How much CO2 is released per unit of electricity generated, usually gCO2/kWh. A low number means cleaner power.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The main way we compare how clean different states’ electricity really is.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The percentage of total electricity generation that comes from renewables (hydro, wind, solar, biomass) plus nuclear.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The headline metric for how much of India’s grid is low-carbon. But it can rise even as coal generation grows.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The combination of fuels used to produce electricity: coal, gas, hydro, nuclear, solar, wind, etc.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Different states have very different mixes, which explains why some are clean and others are coal-heavy.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Coal, natural gas, and oil. Burning them for electricity releases CO2, the main driver of climate change.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Almost all India’s power emissions come from burning coal; gas plays a minor role.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Energy sources that replenish naturally: solar, wind, hydro, and biomass. They produce little or no CO2 when generating electricity.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The growth of renewables, especially solar, is the main reason India’s clean share is rising.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Electricity produced by splitting atoms in a reactor. It is low-carbon but not renewable, and it runs steadily.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Nuclear provides about 3% of India’s electricity and has a high capacity factor (~76%), so it punches above its installed capacity.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The seasonal rainy season in South Asia (June, September). It fills rivers for hydro and drives strong winds.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Explains why hydro and wind output surge in the second half of the year, giving India’s clean power a strong seasonal rhythm.

proseRules: No calculations unless the evidence packet supplies the derived number

evidence.question: Is India's electricity getting cleaner, or just some of India's?

theme.label: Energy and electricity
readerQuestions: How much energy or electricity is being used?
Is access different from reliability or consumption?
Is the system getting cleaner as demand grows?

visualPlan.recommendedVisualization: single-series line chart with latest value and first-to-latest change
visualPlan.mobileGuidance: Use a line chart with large labels, sparse ticks, and a short reader caption.
visualPlan.whyThisMatters: This is the chart that answers the page question most directly.

visualPlan.title: Where India's power-sector emissions actually come from
visualPlan.recommendedVisualization: mobile horizontal bars or selected multi-line chart
visualPlan.mobileGuidance: Prefer horizontal bars, share strips, or at most three lines; avoid dense tables.
visualPlan.whyThisMatters: This is the chart that answers the page question most directly.

plannedCharts.title: How clean is each state's electricity?
plannedCharts.subtitle: Ember India electricity data (compiled from CEA + MNRE) · carbon intensity of power generation · 2024
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The whole argument in one map: there is no single Indian grid, there are thirty, ranging from near-zero-carbon to almost pure coal.

plannedCharts.title: Nationally, the grid really is getting cleaner
plannedCharts.subtitle: Ember · clean (renewables + nuclear) share of India's electricity generation · 2000-2025
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Start with the true national headline before complicating it: the clean share of India's electricity has climbed from about 17% to over a quarter.

plannedCharts.title: But coal kept growing the whole time
plannedCharts.subtitle: Ember · electricity generation by source, India · 2000-2024
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The honest twist: a rising clean share hides the fact that coal generation almost quadrupled, because demand grew faster than clean supply.

plannedCharts.title: Why a megawatt of solar isn't a megawatt of coal
plannedCharts.subtitle: Ember · generation as a share of maximum possible output, by source · India 2024
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The hidden reason capacity and generation tell different stories: intermittent sources run only a fraction of the time.

plannedCharts.title: Three different ways to run a power grid
plannedCharts.subtitle: Ember · share of 2024 generation from each source, by state
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Why states diverge so sharply: a clean grid can mean inherited Himalayan hydro or newly-built wind and solar, and these are completely different maps.

plannedCharts.title: Where India actually built its wind and solar
plannedCharts.subtitle: Ember · installed wind + solar capacity, by state · 2024
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Strip away the inherited hydro and this is the real transition: a handful of states are doing most of the building.

plannedCharts.title: Of the new power added since 2019, how much was clean?
plannedCharts.unit: % of new generation that was clean
plannedCharts.subtitle: Ember · clean share of the rise in generation, 2019 to 2024, by state
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The most decisive test of a transition: when a state needed more electricity, did it reach for clean or for coal?

plannedCharts.title: Power emissions are still rising across most of India
plannedCharts.subtitle: Ember · change in absolute power-sector CO2 emissions, 2019 to 2024
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The sobering counterpoint to a rising clean share: in absolute tonnes, India's power emissions are still going up almost everywhere.

plannedCharts.title: Where India's power emissions actually come from
plannedCharts.subtitle: Ember · absolute power-sector CO2 emissions, by state · 2024
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Intensity tells you how dirty each unit is; this tells you where the tonnes physically come from, which is where decarbonisation has to happen.

plannedCharts.title: The clean grid runs on the monsoon's calendar
plannedCharts.subtitle: Ember · monthly generation from each renewable source · India 2024
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Renewables are not steady: India's clean power has a strong seasonal rhythm that the rest of the grid has to work around.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
